title: Chapter 31: Red
content: The Great Hall was beautiful again.

Candlelight caught the crystal chandeliers and scattered into a thousand fractals across the vaulted ceiling. White linen draped every table, and the centerpiece arrangements—blood-red roses, because of course, because the universe has a sense of humor—bloomed in sterling silver vases. The string quartet in the corner was tuning, thin notes hanging in the air like smoke. The smell of roasting meat and expensive perfume had already begun to layer over the older scent of polished wood and stone that lived in these walls.
Three years ago, I had walked into this same room in white.

White silk, white pearls, white shoes that pinched my toes. White like surrender. White like the flag I'd been taught to wave since birth—the color of a Vance woman who smiled and nodded and made herself small enough to fit inside the margins of someone else's story. I had been nineteen. I had been a girl playing at being decorative.
I was not that girl anymore.

The dress was red. Not the demure burgundy that polite society women wore to charity galas, not the soft rose of a debutante's second-choice gown. This was arterial red. Spinal-cord red. The red that rushes to the surface when skin is broken. It hugged my waist and flared at the hip, and the neckline plunged just low enough to make people stare and just high enough to make them feel guilty about it.

My mother would have been horrified. My mother had been horrified—her voice over the phone that afternoon, thin with the kind of outrage that passes for concern: *Catherine, you cannot be serious. You'll look like—*
Like what, Mother? Like I have a pulse?

I stepped through the archway, and the room shifted. Not dramatically—no one stopped talking, no one dropped their champagne—but I felt the attention move. Little pricks of it, like fingers touching the back of my neck. I smelled the citrus tang of someone's gin and tonic as a waiter passed. I heard the click of my own heels on the marble, steady as a metronome.

Three years ago, this room had been a stage, and I had been performing. Smiling on Robert's arm, laughing at jokes that curdled in my throat, playing the part of the devoted wife-to-be while the boy I loved sat across the room and refused to look at me.
Tonight, the room was not a stage. It was an operating table. And I was the one holding the scalpel.
Julian was already in position.

He stood near the bar, one hand wrapped around a glass of something amber, the other loose at his side. His suit was charcoal gray, immaculate, the tie knotted with the precise symmetry of a man who had been dressing himself for boardrooms since he was fourteen. He looked exactly like what he was pretending to be: the jilted ex, here under protest, fulfilling a family obligation with wounded dignity.

I caught his eye across the room. He raised his glass a fraction of an inch—the tiniest salute. His face gave nothing away, but I knew the code. That slight lift meant: *All systems green. Robert hasn't moved from the board table.*
I tilted my chin a centimeter. *Acknowledged.*

Julian's rage was a beautiful thing. Not the sloppy, loud rage that Robert threw when he was cornered—Julian's was surgical. Contained. He had spent months cultivating the role of the wronged party, letting the board members and the society pages see him as heartbroken, reasonable, a man who'd been discarded. They never suspected that the heartbreak was real but the reason for it was a lie. He wasn't here because I'd left him. He was here because of what had been done to me. To us. To Heath.
Julian Linton had never loved me. But he'd loved the idea of justice long before I'd ever looked at him twice, and that made him more dangerous than anyone in this room understood.

I moved through the crowd, accepting a champagne flute from a passing tray. The bubbles popped against my nose when I brought it to my lips—dry, bright, the taste of money and self-congratulation. A woman whose name I should have remembered greeted me with air kisses and told me my dress was *stunning*, and I smiled and said *thank you* and did not tell her that I'd chosen the color the way a sniper chooses a position: for maximum visibility, minimum forgiveness.
Robert was by the board table.

Of course he was. Robert Vance, golden son of the Vance Financial dynasty, standing with his shoulders back and his chin lifted, holding court with two of the senior board members like he still believed the ground beneath him was solid. He wore his confidence like a second skin—tailored, expensive, impervious to doubt. I could hear the rough warmth of his laugh from fifteen feet away, the way it filled the space around him and made people lean in, made them want to be part of whatever he was selling.
He didn't know.

That was the thing that struck me, standing there with champagne going flat in my hand and the smell of gardenias from someone's corsage turning my stomach. He genuinely didn't know. In his mind, the financial pressure was a problem to be managed, a temporary squeeze that his name and his connections would smooth over before the quarter closed. He still thought this was a world where Richard Vance's son could not fall.

I watched him clap Whitfield on the shoulder. I watched him smile. The sound of his voice—warm, commanding, the voice that had once whispered *you're mine* like it was a promise and not a leash—curled through the room, and I felt nothing. Not anger. Not grief. Just the cold, clean clarity of a woman watching a man walk toward a cliff he couldn't see.

The trap had closed weeks ago. The evidence was stored on three encrypted drives in three different locations. The forensic accounting was airtight. The press contacts were primed. All that remained was the detonation.
And Heath.
My eyes found him before I could stop them—they always did—as if there was a compass in my chest and he was true north.

He was at the head table. Chairman's seat. The position he'd carved out of stone and ice and the kind of willpower that breaks bones. His face was a mask of cold authority, the lines of his jaw set like architecture, his dark eyes scanning the room without settling on anything. He looked carved from the same marble as the columns flanking the stage: beautiful, ancient, indifferent.
But I wasn't looking at his face.
I was looking at his hand.

His right hand held a champagne flute. Just held it—loosely, casually, the way anyone at a gala would hold a glass. Except his fingers were trembling. The smallest tremor, barely visible, the kind of thing you'd miss if you weren't looking for it. The kind of thing you'd miss if you hadn't spent three years memorizing the topography of his hands.
The flute shivered. The champagne quivered inside it.

That tremor was the boy. The boy who had looked at me across this same room three years ago with his whole heart in his eyes and his voice cracking on the word *please*. The boy who had been broken and rebuilt into something razor-edged and lethal—but the break lines were still there, if you knew where to look.
I knew where to look.
I looked away before he could catch me staring.
The forecast had come in that morning.

*Worst blizzard in three years.* That's what every radio announcer and weather app and breathless news anchor had been saying since dawn. The satellite images showed a massive system rolling off the mountains, snow-heavy and mean, with wind gusts that could strip the paint off a car. By midnight, the mountain roads would be impassable—the plows couldn't keep up, and the county had already issued a travel advisory that stopped just short of martial law. By 2 AM, the power lines would go down. The backup generators at Blackwood were good for maybe twelve hours after that, but it wouldn't matter.
Nothing would matter after tonight.
The blizzard wasn't the problem. The blizzard was the plan.

My fingers brushed the outline of my phone in the hidden pocket sewn into the dress's waistline. The evidence was ready. The press packets were ready. Everything—every document, every photograph, every transaction record that proved the board had been bleeding Blackwood's endowment into private accounts for a decade—was loaded and waiting for 10:30 PM, when my thumb would hit send and the whole thing would go public simultaneously to the Times, the Journal, and every major outlet on the eastern seaboard.
An isolated building. No exits. No interruptions. The perfect cage.
I had built it. And I had built it for them.
8 PM.

I checked my watch under the pretense of adjusting my bracelet—a thin gold thing with a broken clasp I'd been meaning to fix for months. The face of it glowed in the ambient light: 8:02 PM. The gala ran until midnight. We had four hours.
The timeline was carved into my brain like scripture.

At 10 PM, Heath would rise from the chairman's seat and begin the financial presentation. It was on the agenda—it had been on the agenda for weeks, listed as the standard quarterly review, unremarkable, procedural. The board expected charts and projections, the usual theater of transparency that let them pretend they had nothing to hide. What they would get instead was the detonation. Every falsified figure, every shell company, every wire transfer that led from Blackwood's endowment straight into Robert Vance's investment accounts and the personal portfolios of every board member who'd looked the other way.
Heath would stand at that podium and burn them. Publicly. Irrevocably. There would be no spin, no backroom deal, no quiet settlement. Just the truth, laid bare in front of three hundred witnesses.

At 10:30, I would release the evidence to the press. The forensic accounting, the bank records, the emails—the entire architecture of the conspiracy, sent from an anonymous account that could never be traced back to me or Heath or Julian. By the time the board realized what was happening, the story would already be live. The internet would have it. The alumni would have it. The donors would have it. There would be no containing it.

At 11, Julian would block the board from calling their lawyers. Not physically—Julian's methods were never physical. He would simply have already preempted them. A word in the right ear, a document filed in the right courthouse, a strategic leak that would make any attorney the board retained a witness to conspiracy. Julian had spent four months weaving that net. The board would reach for their phones and find the lines cut, metaphorically and, in two cases, literally.
Everything was timed. Everything was precise. There was no room for error.

The thought should have been comforting. It wasn't. It sat in my chest like a stone—the weight of all the things that could go wrong, all the variables I couldn't control, all the ways this night could unspool into disaster. But fear was a luxury I'd forfeited the moment I'd put on the red dress.
8:07 PM. One hour and fifty-three minutes until detonation.
I took a breath. The champagne taste was still on my tongue, turning sour.
The window was floor-to-ceiling, antique glass with the faintest ripple of age distorting the view. I stood beside it and watched the snow begin to fall.

It came slowly at first. Single flakes, drifting down from a sky the color of a bruise—grey bleeding into purple bleeding into the black edge of the mountains. They landed on the stone terrace outside and melted on contact, leaving dark wet circles that faded almost as soon as they appeared. The wind had picked up; I could hear it pressing against the glass, a low moan that threaded beneath the noise of the gala like a bass note.

I pressed my fingertips to the cold pane. The glass was thinner than it should have been—old, original to the building, the kind of thing a proper restoration would have replaced. I could feel the chill seep through, sharp and clean, a counterpoint to the heat of the room behind me.

I thought about three years ago. The spring gala. The white dress. The way the snow had come down just like this, soft and quiet and inevitable, while I stood at a window and cried without making a sound because Robert was waiting for me and I couldn't let him see.
I wasn't crying now.
"You're blocking the view," a voice said behind me.

I didn't turn. I didn't need to. I could feel him—the heat of him, the barely perceptible shift in the air pressure when he moved into my space. He stopped beside me, not touching, just standing close enough that I could feel the warmth radiating off his body through the wool of his suit jacket.
He didn't look at me. He looked at the snow.
"It's starting," he said.

Two words. A whole language. I knew he meant the blizzard—the first flakes of the storm that would lock us all inside this building until morning. I also knew he meant the fire—the first sparks of the inferno we'd spent months building, the one that would consume the board and the corruption and the entire rotting infrastructure of power that had nearly destroyed us both.
I watched a flake land on the outside sill and hold its shape for one second—crystalline, perfect—before the window's warmth melted it into nothing.
"Good," I said.

His hand moved. Just the smallest motion, his little finger shifting until it was a millimeter from mine on the windowsill. Not touching. Not quite. But the intention was there, electric and deliberate, a message transmitted through air: *I'm here. I see you. We burn together.*
I didn't close the gap. Neither did he. The distance between our hands was the only honest thing in this room.

Then the orchestra began to play—a waltz, something European and mournful, the kind of piece that made you think of ballrooms in decaying palaces—and a voice came over the PA system, polite and polished, announcing that dinner was served and guests should take their seats.

The crowd began to move. The subtle, choreographed migration of wealthy people toward food—champagne flutes carried like shields, laughter trailing behind them like perfume. I watched them go, the men in their dark suits and the women in their jewel tones, sapphires and emeralds and amethysts, all of them glittering like cut glass.

Heath's hand retreated. His warmth pulled back like a tide going out, and I felt the absence of it like a wound. When I turned, he was already walking toward the head table, his stride measured and unhurried, the chairman returning to his throne.
I followed the current of guests into the dining room.

The doors closed behind us. I heard them—the heavy, deliberate sound of oak on stone, the click of the latch falling into place. Not locked. Just closed. But the sound was final nonetheless. The sound of a room sealing itself. The sound of a trap springing shut.

I took my seat. The place card read CATHERINE VANCE in copperplate script, and I almost laughed at the formality of it—the idea that I was still Catherine to these people, still the woman they'd dismissed, still the ornament.

The first plate was served. Seared scallop on a bed of microgreens, the butter scent rising in a warm curl, the edge of the shell perfectly caramelized. Someone to my left remarked on the presentation. Someone to my right refilled my wine glass. The crystal clinked. The silverware gleamed.

Robert stood from his seat near the board table and raised his glass. "To Blackwood," he said, his voice ringing with the effortless charm that had fooled me once and fooled these people still. "And to another year of excellence."
"To Blackwood," the room echoed.

Three hundred voices. Three hundred people who had looked through me, used me, underestimated me. Three hundred people who had watched me disappear into the margins of my own life and never once wondered if I was drowning.
I raised my glass with the rest of them.
The wine was red. Of course it was.

I looked around the room—at Heath's still face at the head table, at Julian's bowed head at the bar, at Robert's confident smile, at the snow piling against the windows in thick white drifts, at the clock on the far wall that I could see from where I sat.
The minute hand moved.
One hour until detonation.
I set my glass down and reached for my knife, and I thought: *You built a cage around me. You made me into something ornamental. You forgot that ornaments can cut.*
The blizzard was coming. The clock was ticking.
And there was no way out for anyone.
Including me.
